data.title: LTCN: Spot Litecoin ETFs May Be Weeks Away
data.description:
In the roughly three months since I last covered Litecoin (LTC-USD) and the Grayscale Litecoin Trust (OTCQX:LTCN) for Seeking Alpha, the performances of both the coin and the fund have been admittedly lack-luster.

When I wrote my last piece, LTCN was trading at a small premium relative to the prior year. As of mid-April, not only is that premium gone entirely but the fund has shifted back to a NAV rate discount for the first time since Q4-23. In this update, we'll look at the latest pertaining to spot Litecoin ETFs, Litecoin's continued usage story, and why I still like both LTC and LTCN given the broader setup in the digital asset market.

Spot Litecoin ETF Developments

While it isn't yet exactly clear when (or even if) spot ETFs will be approved by the SEC, the indications are that they will be coming at some point in the future. Consider that Canary Capital's Litecoin ETF is currently listed on the Depository Trust & Clearing Corporation's website as a 'Pre Launch' ticker:

This doesn't actually mean that LTCC is guaranteed to become an approved ETF, but it is a positive sign as it shows the issuer is becoming more prepared for approval.

Bloomberg ETF analysts Eric Balchunas and James Seyffart previously put LTC ETF approval odds at 90%. Furthermore, Seyffart said in early-February that he believes LTC will be the next ETF approved in the United States. In March, the SEC delayed a decision on the Canary fund and the next deadline is May 5th, which as of article submission, is less than three weeks away.

Revisiting The Litecoin Thesis

A view that I've steadfastly held in my coverage of cryptocurrencies through the years is the idea that the networks where these assets live have to actually offer the world real utility. Otherwise, speculation on price is likely the only real point of existence. That's not the future the cypherpunks who originally advocated for Bitcoin (BTC-USD) envisioned. But to a large degree, it's indeed the reality that we have today. That doesn't mean it has to remain the case going forward, and I've long suspected that through the bullish/bearish cycles that crypto speculators endure, the assets that will ultimately remain are the ones that offer the very utility I mentioned.

There are many in the space who will argue that Bitcoin can do just about anything. BTC is viewed as 'Digital Gold' and any end user payments for smaller transactions can simply happen on secondary scaling layers. Thus, LTC need not exist. Yet lo and behold, after over 13 years in the market, Litecoin still commands a market capitalization of nearly $6 billion and a market cap rank of 21. For a coin that some feel is totally obsolete, this is impressive staying power, in my view. And my thinking here is the reason LTC still matters is precisely because scaling Bitcoin efforts have stagnated while Litecoin usage continues to grow.

I've shared this chart in prior articles, but I'm sharing it again because I believe it helps illustrate two things; first, Litecoin's 30-day average DAA trend remains up since the beginning of 2021 while Bitcoin's is actually down. Second, when Bitcoin's transaction fees spiked in late-2023 and early-2024, crypto's user base shifted to Litecoin. I think another way to look at this though is through what I'll call the BTC/LTC DAA Multiple:

This logarithmic chart shows Bitcoin DAAs divided by Litecoin DAAs on a rolling 90-day average. This is essentially another way of showing Bitcoin losing active user share to Litecoin since 2017. Back then, the BTC/LTC DAA multiple was 60x. Today, it's just 2x, and it even spent some of 2024 under 2x. This isn't the only multiple that I think matters. You won't get any argument from me that Bitcoin settles far more in USD-denominated value each day. Over the last 30 days, Bitcoin has averaged about $9 billion in daily transferred value, while Litecoin's figure is closer to $86 million. To be sure, a massive difference. But adjusting for the differences in market capitalization, LTC is far cheaper than BTC:

This is the 30-day average NVT ratio; which divides the market capitalization of the coin by the USD-denominated transferred value on the network. I've added Dogecoin (DOGE-USD) and Bitcoin Cash (BCH-USD) to show that LTC is indeed the outlier. At a 77 NVT ratio, Litecoin's valuation continues to trade at a massive discount to Proof-of-Work payment chain peers. DOGE, BCH, and BTC all command NVT ratios in excess of 200. So this is either a bad framework, or Litecoin is undervalued relative to the rest of the market. I continue to believe the issue lies with the valuations rather than the framework.

Grayscale Litecoin Trust

I'll point out again that my issue with LTCN as an investment in recent years hasn't been due to the underlying asset or its attractiveness as a digital currency. My problem with the fund over the last year and change has simply been one of valuation. At a significant multiple over the underlying value of the LTC, LTCN isn't worth the price tag. However, at a discount, I like it a lot more due to what, I think, is discounted exposure to a digital asset that is also significantly discounted relative to Proof-of-Work peers.

And that's exactly what we have from the market today. LTCN trades a small discount to NAV. And LTC trades a significant discount to the NVT ratios that are presently observed by natural peers BTC, BCH, and DOGE. All this said, a consideration that LTCN shareholders should remain mindful of is share unlocks through the end of the year:

Grayscale held 1.48 million LTC through the trust back in February 2024. By the end of April, LTC holdings grew to 1.69 million. Though as of April 17th, most of the shares issued from that LTC growth are already unlocked. The next big unlock appears to be in mid-July, where LTC holdings grew by 5% overnight. LTC pricing likely matters here because between April 15th, 2024 and July 17th, 2024, Litecoin traded between $60-80 dollars per coin. Which means that some of these LTCN shares are likely already underwater. Will those shareholders take the loss and further depress the NAV rate? I won't attempt to guess.

Closing Thoughts

Litecoin is quietly still an important asset in the cryptocurrency market. It's one of just three coins that can be purchased through a Fidelity crypto IRA, and all indications from ETF analysts are that the spot LTC ETFs will be approved in the United States. When that happens, LTCN's days at a discount are presumably over. So coin price being equal, buying LTCN at a discount could be a nice little arbitrage trade between now and a potential ETF conversion. Fundamentally, LTC remains highly depressed relative to similar assets. If LTC traded at the same NVT ratio as BTC, the coin price would be closer to $225.

data.title: OPTZ: An Optimistic All-Cap ETF With A Simple But Unique Strategy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Optimize Strategy Index ETF (NASDAQ:OPTZ) from Optimize Financial is a really interesting rules-based fund I recently came across that's based on the firm's unwavering belief in the growth potential of innovative American companies. While this belief is required for any U.S. equity ETF to a degree, OPTZ's beauty is in its simplicity, as it screens large-cap stocks using just three metrics and small-cap stocks on only one metric. While I have some concerns about its sector composition and safety qualities, I'm hoping readers can appreciate its uniqueness. Though I'm not ready to assign a "buy" rating today, OPTZ is worth watching, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

OPTZ Overview

Strategy Discussion and Index Methodology

OPTZ tracks the Optimize Strategy Index, which launched on November 30, 2023, and has a base date of March 15, 2023. I've summarized its strategy in the seven bullet points below.

1. The Index objective is to generate excess total returns as compared to the Optimize Broad Market Index. This benchmark index employs a modified market cap-weighted approach.

2. The benchmark index is the eligible universe and requires companies to have a market capitalization of roughly $850 million. The Index also applies basic liquidity screens and excludes securities structured as ETFs, CEFs, ADRs, Preferred Shares, Warrants, Rights, MLPs, Mineral Royalty Companies, Oil & Gas Royalty Companies, and Investment Trusts.

3. Securities are classified along the following top-level categorizations:

  • Banking, Investing & Insurance: Financials
  • Bits & Bytes: Technology
  • Building Blocks: Basic Materials
  • Communication & Media Services
  • Elastic Spending: Consumer Discretionary
  • Inelastic Spending: Consumer Staples
  • Nuts & Bolts: Industrials
  • Oil, Gas & Others: Energy
  • Pharma & Healthcare: Health Care
  • Real Estate
  • Water & Power (Utilities)

4. Eligible securities are ranked within their respective sectors and size segments. The Index considers large-cap companies those with market caps above $2 billion, with ranking criteria and weights as follows:

  • Current EPS / Trailing 10-Year Average EPS: 50%
  • Current Price / Earnings Ratio: 25%
  • Forward Two-Year EPS Growth Estimate: 25%

Those with market caps below $2 billion are considered small-cap companies and are ranked solely using the Price / Sales ratio.

5. The top quintile (20%) of companies are selected from each market capitalization segment in each sector.

6. The Index weights securities in two steps. First, sector allocations are determined based on the total market cap of stocks in a particular sector as a percentage of the total market cap of all securities. Then, stocks within each sector are weighted equally.

7. The Index reconstitutes semi-annually after business close on the third Friday in March and September.

As you can tell, OPTZ's strategy is pretty simple, as it takes a growth-at-a-reasonable-price approach for large-cap stocks and a value-only approach for small-cap stocks. To ensure adequate small-cap stock representation, the Index weights securities equally but only within their own sector, and it's this last point I find most intriguing. The creators could have just weighted all securities equally. However, they decided to allow mega-cap stocks like Nvidia (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL) influence how much weight gets assigned to other Technology stocks, even if they themselves aren't the best opportunities.

Sector Allocations and Peer Group

This weighting scheme could represent the firm's bias toward the sectors dominated by these mega-cap stocks (Technology, Communication Services, Consumer Discretionary). To illustrate, by equal-weighting all members of the fund's 303-stock portfolio, Technology exposure would total just 13% vs. 38% under the current method. That's a big difference for what amounts to only a footnote in the strategy description. Below are OPTZ's sector allocations against four other all-cap funds you might find interesting, and as you can see, OPTZ overweights Consumer Discretionary and Technology the most, with offsets in defensive sectors like Consumer Staples and Health Care.

OPTZ has a 0.50% expense ratio, which is competitive to MOAT and MOTE, though also has a 0.50% median bid-ask spread, so it's not exactly a cheap fund to trade. I think the Schwab Fundamental U.S. Broad Market ETF (FNDB) is the type of fund OPTZ seeks to outperform, but I also want to evaluate Fidelity's new Enhanced U.S. All-Cap Equity ETF (FEAC), which has yet to garner any interest (only $4.56 million in AUM), but ranks well on my end fundamentally and has a cheap 0.18% expense ratio.

OPTZ Analysis

Performance Snapshot

Since OPTZ launched on April 22, 2024, it has delivered a 2.46% total return, which is about 3-5% better than VanEck's Wide Moat ETFs. However, it lagged behind FNDB by almost 2%, so at least based on this short track record, the simpler fund is still better.

However, OPTZ's Index does provide backtested results for ten years prior to the launch date. After calculating monthly returns and adjusting for expenses, I found the Index would have outperformed FNDB from September 2013 to April 2025. I say "would have" because these results have the benefit of hindsight, and frankly, it's extremely unusual to see poor backtested results. Nevertheless, here is a chart showing the growth of $10K for each.

OPTZ slightly edged out FNDB on the growth of $10K ($36,829 vs. $34,263), but annualized over 140 months, the difference is pretty small (11.82% vs. 11.13%). What's more interesting is the excess volatility highlighted in years like 2020 and 2022. While FNDB's annualized standard deviation is moderate at 15.52%, OPTZ's was much higher at 20.03%, and it also had an inferior "worst month" figure (-26.99% vs. -16.87%). These are clues that OPTZ might not deliver good risk-adjusted returns moving forward.

OPTZ Fundamental Analysis

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for OPTZ's top 25 sub-industries, which, I felt, was appropriate because, due to the weighting scheme, its top 25 holdings consist mainly of tech stocks. This view lets us quickly see how Systems Software, Semiconductors, and Application Software stocks comprise about one-quarter of the portfolio, but it's still well-diversified, with just 65.44% allocated to the top 25.

Here are three additional observations to consider:

OPTZ: More Evidence It's A High-Risk Fund

OPTZ's five-year portfolio beta is 1.27, indicating a substantial level of market risk. While the addition of small-cap stocks usually makes funds more volatile, that's not the only cause, as other sub-industries with $100+ billion market caps have high betas, including:

  • Semiconductors: 1.61
  • Application Software: 1.32
  • Movies & Entertainment: 1.59
  • Interactive Media & Services: 2.20

One of the reasons I included MOAT and MOTE in this analysis is because they also have market caps of around $150 billion, yet the two Morningstar-Indexed funds managed to get their betas down close to one. MOAT, for example, has 6.63% allocated to semiconductors, but its selections are comparatively less risky and have betas averaging 1.36 instead of OPTZ's 1.61. MOAT also overweight less risky segments like Aerospace & Defense, making it a safer play when things don't go well.

OPTZ: GARP Features Are Excellent

OPTZ is extremely well-designed from a GARP perspective. As shown above, it trades at 14.77x forward earnings and 9.78x trailing cash flow, which is about the same as FNDB. However, OPTZ also features almost twice the estimated earnings growth over the next twelve months (14.36% vs. 8.70%) and is challenged only by FEAC, which has a higher growth profile (i.e., higher growth rates and higher valuations).

Notice that nearly every sub-industry has double-digit estimated EPS growth. Historical sales growth is also solid, as OPTZ's holdings have increased sales by 11.26% per year over the last five years. That's nice to see, given how the Index doesn't even screen for it. As shown below, OPTZ's growth and value metrics are more attractive across the board compared to the peer group average, and if the highlighted growth and value scores (derived from Seeking Alpha Factor Grades) are any indications, it's the most attractive GARP fund of the five.

OPTZ: Small Caps and Weighting Scheme Negatively Impact Quality

OPTZ has the lowest sector-adjusted profit score at 7.90/10, whereas the four peers listed have scores above 8.50/10. That's backed up by OPTZ's lower net margins (12.59%) and free cash flow margins (13.23%), and it's not that surprising because the Index doesn't have any direct quality screens. This allows more unprofitable stocks to sneak their way into the fund and receive a higher weighting than they would otherwise. For example, 39/303 holdings in OPTZ (13.80% by weight) have negative net margins compared to 264/1657 (5.26% by weight) for FNDB.

The Index uses a $2 billion breakpoint to separate large- and small-cap stocks, but I was surprised to find only 51/303 stocks below this threshold. Although relatively small in number, these stocks drag down the portfolio's quality. As shown below, they have only 1.00% net margins on average, compared to 14.02% for those above $2 billion.

In addition, OPTZ's weighting scheme negatively impacts quality. Consider that if OPTZ cap-weighted its selections and applied a simple 2% limit on mega-cap stocks, its profit score would rise from 7.90/10 to 8.92/10. However, that would lower Technology sector exposure from 38% to 21%, and I think that goes against the firm's general outlook. Optimize Financial is simply bullish on the sector long-term, and while there's nothing wrong with that, I don't agree with boosting the weights of smaller tech companies simply because the sector is home to giants like Apple and Nvidia.

Investment Recommendation

OPTZ's best features are its 14.36% estimated growth rate and its 14.77x forward P/E ratio. Compared to the peers evaluated today, this combination is the best, and I'm impressed the team was able to deliver this while screening for only four metrics. That said, the fund faces challenges because its holdings are higher risk, evidenced by a 1.27 five-year portfolio beta and an 11+year backtest that revealed higher drawdowns and greater volatility. In addition, OPTZ includes some smaller companies and boosts the weight of some lower-quality stocks through a unique weighting scheme, which could also weigh on returns in periods of uncertainty like today. For these reasons, I've assigned OPTZ a "hold" rating, but if you're an optimistic GARP investor looking for a simple but unique ETF, I'd certainly add OPTZ to your watch list. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to your comments below.

data.title: TBUX: Continues To Be A Buy In A Liquidity Constrained World
data.description:
Thesis

We last covered the T. Rowe Price Ultra Short-Term Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:TBUX) last year, when we rated this short-term bond fund from T. Rowe a 'Buy'. A number of things have happened since - namely a market decline of roughly -10%, followed by a brief respite, only to be followed by an enormous week of volatility and another -12% drawdown. In light of the liquidity and drawdown issues faced by other funds, we are going to revisit TBUX and show investors why this name has been so robust and continues to warrant a 'Buy' rating.

What are cash like parking vehicles supposed to do

Cash like vehicles are supposed to have de-minimis volatility and offer a distribution yield comparable with T-bills. More often than not, this cohort tends to offer slightly higher yield via an offset in other risks. Those risks are duration, credit risk and liquidity.

A robust cash parking vehicle is supposed to weather volatility very well, providing for a 'cash-like' instrument. As a reminder, if you hold your savings in cash or money market funds, there are no drawdowns. While yield enhancement does not come without risks, some funds have delivered in the past month, whereas others have shown issues. Let us have a look.

Some names have disappointed

Let us have a look at a cohort of cash like vehicles and their performance in the past month:

Without knowing anything about these names, something should stand out here - we have two cohort of funds. On one hand, the SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-bill ETF (BIL) and TBUX, while the second cohort is represented by the NEOS Enhanced Income ETF (CSHI) and the Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA). JAAA and CSHI had drawdowns of close to -1.8%, whereas BIL and TBUX did not.

Yield enhancement involves taking other risks, as mentioned above. JAAA takes liquidity risk, while CSHI takes equity risk. Both those risks resulted in a drawdown. An investor needs to ask themselves if they are willing to have a -1.8% drawdown for 1% more in yield enhancement. Some do like that equation, some don't. We like TBUX and its performance because it delivered in the past month. The fund had a drawdown sub -0.4%, and is almost flat again. This is exactly how cash like vehicle is supposed to perform. It is expected to have a small drawdown, but nothing above -1%.

Construction and holdings play a role

The reasons for which TBUX did not collapse have to do with its construction. The fund is overweight corporate bonds and treasuries, the most liquid instruments in the fixed income world:

While even treasuries saw some liquidity issues via wider bid/ask spreads, overall corporates and treasuries held their marks when faced with volatility and risk off. CLOs via JAAA have shown us that there is a cost for liquidity, and the market for CLOs is not as deep as the one for corporates.

From a construction standpoint, TBUX is further buttressed by its very short duration of 0.58 years which does not allow a lot of credit spread slippage even when the markets are stressed. The name contains only investment grade bonds:

As we can see from the above table, the vast majority of the holdings are investment grade ('IG'), with the lowest level of IG (i.e. BBB names) at only 34% of the fund. An investor has to be mindful of funds which have 50% or more BBB names, since they start running increased credit risk. While we do not believe in actual defaults, increased credit risk translates into price slippage during a market sell-off.

Analytics

The fund's analytics stack out as follows:

  • AUM: $0.42 billion
  • Expense ratio: 0.17%
  • Yield: 4.68% (30-day SEC yield)
  • Premium/Discount to NAV: 0%
  • Risk: Standard Deviation 1.28% (3 year)
  • Type: Fixed Income ETF - Short Duration

Nothing is free in the financial world

A retail investor needs to understand that nothing is free in finance. If a fund yields 100 bps more than T-Bills, then it takes some sort of risk. TBUX yields roughly 40 bps over T-Bills, but its duration and credit risks are very well contained. As we have seen from the sections above, other funds have done much, much worse. Now, a retail investor does not need to worry about those funds either if holding for longer term, but what one tends to do is panic at the bottom. And when panic is sweeping the markets, the last thing you want to see is your cash parking vehicles down -1.8%. TBUX has passed that test, and should represent a very safe choice going forward, even if a recession hits.

Let us also look at TBUX versus other corporate bond (short term) funds:

All corporate bond funds have done very well, as we can see from the above graph. We have covered most of the names from this cohort, a cohort which includes the PIMCO Enhanced Short Maturity Active ETF (MINT), the iShares Short Duration Bond Active ETF (NEAR) and the JPMorgan Ultra-Short Income ETF (JPST). The lesson here? Corporate bonds and treasuries have the most liquidity in a severe risk-off scenario, and they tend to hold up the best.

Conclusion

TBUX is a fixed income exchange-traded fund. The name is a short-term cash parking vehicle, and contains mostly corporate bonds and treasuries. The fund has done very well in the past month, unlike other yield enhancing vehicles, having a drawdown under -0.5%. The market has taught us that liquidity risk and equity risk have a price, and that price is a drawdown of almost -1.8% as other names have exhibited. We like TBUX's construction and performance, with a continued 'Buy' versus taking liquidity or equity risk via other vehicles.

data.title: Uber: Lifting My Price Target As Shares Circle About, Neutral Chart
data.description:
Few stocks have been left unscathed by this year's turmoil, particularly those related to tech. Uber (NYSE:UBER), on the other hand, is up more than 21% so far in 2025, sharply outperforming the S&P 500 (SPY), which is down by more than 10%, dividends included.

Ahead of the ride-hailing company's Q1 earnings report in early May, I reiterate a buy rating. I am lifting my price target, but will acknowledge a neutral absolute chart despite UBER's impressive relative strength in recent months. Its Waymo launch in Austin appears to be going well, and hope abounds for the Atlanta entrance this week.

Since my previous analysis in July 2024, the stock is up 2.5%, outperforming the S&P 500's -5.5% loss.

Uber Sharply Beating the S&P 500 in 2025, Shares +132% Since the Start of 2024

Back in February, Uber, now ranked No. 1 in its industry per Seeking Alpha's quantitative scoring system, reported a strong set of quarterly results. Q4 GAAP EPS of $3.21 was not comparable to the $0.48 Wall Street consensus estimate, but sales growth of more than 20% from the same period a year earlier was a notable $190 million beat. Gross bookings soared by 18% from Q4 of 2023, a 21% constant currency advance. Trip sales during the October through December stretch rose 18% to 3.1 billion. Shares plunged by 7.3% in the session that followed, however, marking a second straight negative reaction to a quarterly report.

Looking ahead to the May 7 Q1 earnings update, the options market prices in a 7.3% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the reporting date. The company has topped bottom-line estimates in each of the previous three instances.

In terms of the most recent update, there was solid strength in both mobile gross bookings and delivery. The latter grew by 18%. Unfortunately, EBITDA was a bit underwhelming, which perhaps led to the bearish stock price reaction. That earnings figure was largely the result of significant long-term capex, so the management team sees many growth avenues, which is an encouraging sign, despite near-term volatility.

It expanded its Uber One subscriptions to 30 million worldwide, with notable gains in Uber Teen rides and Uber for Business. Mentioned earlier, Waymo is progressing well, which bodes well for the firm's position in the broader automotive vehicle market.

For Q1, the management team expects gross bookings between $42 and $43.5 billion, and that assumes a 5.5 percentage point currency headwind. A weaker dollar lately could lead to an EPS boost in the coming quarters, though. The guidance hike was a hopeful sign given the growth momentum-the firm is confident that it can achieve mid to high teens EPS growth rates in the years to come.

On the earnings outlook, solid growth is expected over the next 12 months and through the out year. Operating EPS may come in above $2.50 with a massive bottom-line growth pace through FY 2027. All the while, revenue growth is seen steady between 14% and 15%.

UBER is among the most covered stocks on the street right now, and after the healthy Q4, there have been a high 34 sellside EPS upgrades compared to just 11 downgrades. On the profitability front, the company sports solid metrics-free cash flow per share is $3.29, which is a 4.5% FCF yield.

Uber: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have updates since my mid-$80s target last summer. If we assume normalized EPS of $2.70 and apply a 33x P/E, then shares should trade near $89. I arrive at that multiple due to its long-term EPS growth rate, which may come in above 20% based on the next two years' trends.

What's more, $89 is just 21 times FY 2027 EPS, so the growth pace warrants a lofty current P/E in my view. UBER also trades inexpensively on a price-to-sales basis based on its historical trends. On a free cash flow basis, UBER is cheap compared to the other mega-cap tech-related stocks as well.

UBER Now With a Cheap PEG Ratio, P/S Retreats, Big FCF

A key risk with UBER today is how the management team executes Waymo and the broader AV commercialization strategy. Competition is high, including from Tesla (TSLA). Industry pressures could also result in softer margins going forward, particularly if consumer and business travel drops amid a US recession later this year. Rising insurance costs are another potential headwind. Finally, adverse regulatory outcomes and higher state minimum wages could hurt profitability.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q1 2025 earnings date of Wednesday, May 7 BMO, with a conference call immediately after the numbers hit the tape.

Two days before the report, Uber hosts its Annual General Meeting, so the first full week of May could be pivotal for the stock, which has traded sideways for several months.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With a higher intrinsic value target compared to at the start of the third quarter of 2024, UBER's technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that there's an obvious range of support and resistance. Buyers have stepped up to the plate at and below the $60 mark, while sellers have emerged on a few occasions on trips above $80. Moreover, the RSI momentum gauge at the top of the graph has hit both technical overbought and oversold conditions in the last five months, and it's almost exactly neutral now.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average-it's merely flat in its slope, suggesting a battle between the bulls and bears, with price moving above and below that trend-indicator line. There's a high amount of volume by price from $60 to $85, which tells me that there's a significant congestion zone. A breakout above the all-time high would yield an upside measured move price objective to about $120 based on the height of the current range.

Overall, the technicals are neutral, but I am impressed with UBER's alpha to the S&P 500 so far in 2025.

UBER: Shares Rangebound With Neutral RSI Trends, Significant YTD Alpha

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on UBER. I see the $155 billion market cap Industrials sector stock as attractive on valuation given the growth path, while the technical chart continues to churn, which is better than most other large-cap tech-adjacent company shares.

data.title: DGI For The DIY: 2024 Dividend Portfolio Review
data.description:
I've been investing in stocks since 2005 and writing about this portfolio since 2013. During those twenty years, I've learned many things, but none more important than the realization that I cannot time the market.

Fortunately, I've learned to ignore the little voices of doubt in my head that say, "A recession is coming", "Stocks are too expensive", or "The election is going to crater stocks".

Yes, stocks go down occasionally, but the trend over the past hundred-plus years is higher, and that trend continued in 2024.

So, I continue to be a pretty boring portfolio manager. I buy high-quality stocks, hold them for long periods, and collect growing dividends that provide seed money for building new positions.

The following is a report on how that strategy played out for my dividend growth portfolio in 2024.

Stock Market Overview

The stock market in 2024 was the little engine that could.

Wars, interest rates, or politics didn't matter, the market indices continued marching higher.

Despite the chatter about an imminent recession when 2024 began, all three major indices produced double-digit gains for the year, led by the Nasdaq (IXIC) at 28.64%. The S&P 500 (SPX) was close behind with a 23.31% gain, while The Dow Jones Industrials (DJI) returned a respectable 12.88%.

Portfolio Performance

While it didn't quite match the performance of the Nasdaq or S&P, the "DGI For The DIY" portfolio also had a productive year. It produced a 15.68% gain and reached the $150k portfolio value for the first time before closing the year slightly below that mark.

Various holdings drove those gains, as eight companies from six sectors produced 30%+ gains for the year. Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) was the runaway winner in the portfolio, more than doubling in price. With the increase, it's now more than double the second-largest position and is worth 9.3% of the portfolio.

Not all holdings were winners, however. Six companies saw double-digit losses in 2024, hurting the portfolio's overall returns. The biggest losses came from CVS Health Corp. (CVS) at -43.15% and Dollar General Corp. (DG) at -44.23%.

It's never fun watching your stocks fall in price, but the price weakness provided some nice buying opportunities during the year, which I took advantage of with multiple purchases.

Portfolio Guidelines

Before we get into the details of those transactions, let's look at how the portfolio is managed. This is a dividend growth portfolio, so its primary focus is a growing stream of dividend income. Here are the general guidelines I use to accomplish that goal.

  • Buy companies that consistently show positive growth in earnings and translate those earnings into increasing dividend payouts to shareholders.
  • Focus on investment-grade companies with BBB or higher S&P credit ratings.
  • Maintain a diversified portfolio spread across multiple industries.
  • Consider for sale any company that cuts or freezes its dividend.
  • Collect and reinvest dividends into more shares of companies that I own.

This portfolio produces a passive income stream that will help fund a portion of my future retirement. At forty-six years old, I have roughly twenty years until I reach that goal.

I switched to the DGI strategy in 2013, and in late 2017, I added the goal of 10% annual income growth for the portfolio. I finished that year with $2,005 in dividend income and calculated that with a 10% annual income growth rate, this portfolio could produce over $26,000 in dividend income in 2044 when I turn 66.

Here is my progress so far:

The portfolio remains ahead of the pace for income growth, although the gap has narrowed over the last two years. Slower growth from the consumer staples, energy, and real estate sectors, and a change from automatic to selective dividend reinvestment, dropped income growth below 10% in 2023 and again in 2024.

In recent years, I've been shifting my focus more to growth than income with new investments, which should pay off in the form of higher capital appreciation and dividend growth on the new investments. However, this comes through reinvesting dividends from higher-income stocks into lower-yielding ones, which reduces income growth in the short term.

Having the scorecard is a good way to keep my long-term goals focused, but I don't want to overreact to a down year or two by making wholesale changes to the portfolio. Businesses operate in cycles depending on economic conditions, interest rates, and geopolitics, so what's a laggard now can become a leader when the cycle changes.

That said, two years in a row below the target is concerning, and if the trend continues, more significant changes may be needed.

Dividend Income Summary

As mentioned, the portfolio again fell short of the 10% income growth goal for 2024, the second consecutive year below that target.

Dividend income grew by 4.79% in 2024, an even smaller increase than the 6.57% growth in 2023.

A major factor in this reduced growth rate has been EOG Resources (EOG), whose earnings have suffered from lower crude oil prices, resulting in lower dividend payouts. While the company has continued increasing its regular dividend, it has stopped paying special dividends.

Over the past three years, EOG's special dividend payouts in the portfolio dropped from $183.51 in 2022 to $79.10 in 2023 and zero in 2024. This factor alone accounted for a 2.4% income growth headwind in 2023 and a 2.1% headwind in 2024.

This ~2% headwind will be gone in 2025, making the 10% growth target more achievable.

Dividend Increase Announcements

This portfolio holds 54 companies, meaning there have been many dividend growth announcements over the past year. Here are the dividend increase announcements recorded in the portfolio in 2024.

The average annual increase across the portfolio was 7.33%, and the average dividend yield was 2.83%, resulting in a Chowder Number of 10.16%.

Notable increases came from Bank OZK (OZK), Watsco (WSO), Texas Roadhouse (TXRH), NextEra Energy (NEE), UnitedHealth Group (UNH), Microsoft (MSFT), Visa (V), and T-Mobile US (TMUS), which all boosted dividends by double-digit rates.

I started adding T-Mobile to the portfolio in May, so it was great to see that my investment was already paying off when it announced a massive 35.38% dividend increase in September. The company's yield is just 1.48%, but mid-teens annual growth should quickly compound that number.

Laggards include STAG Industrial (STAG), T. Rowe Price Group (TROW), Agree Realty (ADC), and Realty Income (O), which all grew dividends by less than 3%.

Higher interest rates have dramatically slowed the growth rates of real estate companies. In addition to the small increases noted above, American Tower Corp. (AMT), Digital Realty Trust (DLR), and Omega Healthcare Investors (OHI) held their payouts flat in 2024, further hurting the portfolio's income growth.

2024 Portfolio Sales

It was a fairly quiet year for portfolio trades as the only sales were a trim of Broadcom before its 10:1 split in February, the sale of NXP Semi (NXPI), and trims of Apple (AAPL) and Watsco in November.

The Broadcom sale was a small trim of a position that has grown into the largest piece in the portfolio. Broadcom was purchased in February 2019 as a replacement for International Business Machines (IBM). Since then, it has grown to a >9% portfolio weighting, even after the trimming of shares.

Shares have continued marching higher after the sale, so the move doesn't look very smart in hindsight. However, it now trades at more than twice its historical PE multiple, and I felt diversifying and taking some profits was prudent portfolio management.

NXP was sold to swap positions back into Qualcomm (QCOM). NXP hasn't raised the dividend since January 2023 and is expected to see declining earnings for the third straight year in 2025.

Apple and Watsco were trimmed based on valuation concerns and declining dividend growth. Apple hasn't had a double-digit dividend increase since 2018, and it trades with a low 30s multiple, which seems rich compared to its earnings growth rate.

Watsco trades at 37X 2024 estimates and, despite the trim, remains the #4 position in the portfolio.

Trimming these freed up funds to fill out two other positions that offered similar total dividend payouts, with a bit more expected income growth.

2024 Portfolio Purchases

Twenty-one purchases of thirteen different companies were made in the portfolio in 2024. While most were add-on purchases of companies I already owned, new buys were made in Nexstar Media Group, Inc. (NXST), T-Mobile US, Inc., and Qualcomm Inc. (QCOM).

Three add-on purchases were made in Starbucks (SBUX), which had a rough 2024 due to high coffee prices, weakness in China, and some missteps in business operations. Earnings dropped by 6% in 2024 and are expected to drop another 12% in 2025 before rebounding to double-digit growth thereafter.

These buys bring Starbucks to a full position in the portfolio, which is warranted based on the company's history as a consistent earnings and dividend grower.

Nexstar Media Group is a new addition that is a bit more speculative than some others I own. It is a media company that produces and distributes local and national news, sports, and entertainment content in the US.

The company is a dividend challenger that has grown dividends for twelve consecutive years and by over 25% annually over the last decade. The company also has an aggressive share repurchase program that has reduced the share count from 46 million to 33 million since 2019.

Its earnings are choppy due to a volatile advertising market that relies on big events like the US elections and two-year Olympic cycles, but I like the trend of growth over time and believe it could be a nice long-term hold for the portfolio.

The NextEra Energy and Xcel Energy (XEL) buys were opportunistic purchases made during the utility sell-off that occurred early this year.

I added to both companies at some of the lowest prices seen for either stock in the past four years, which has already resulted in some nice capital appreciation. Additionally, these buys raised their weightings in the portfolio closer to full weight.

T-Mobile is another fast grower I added in an attempt to shift focus from income to growth in the portfolio. The company initiated a dividend at the end of 2023 and announced a 35.4% dividend increase in September. With earnings growth expected in the mid-teens, this should be another good long-term hold.

End of 2024 Portfolio Holdings

Taking all of those trades into account, this is how the portfolio stood at year's end.

Broadcom's big year moved it up to a 9.30% weighting in the portfolio, which is nearly 3X the size of the second-largest holdings, Microsoft and Lowe's, at 3.38%.

Broadcom comes in at #8 for dividend income with a 3.40% weighting, while Altria leads the way with a 5.48% weighting.

Looking at the list of top income producers, Broadcom was the only position that produced a 10%+ dividend increase in 2024, while several of the big payers produced low single-digit or zero growth. This highlights the difficulty in reaching double-digit income growth for the portfolio.

Here are the overall portfolio weightings by sector for valuation and income.

The Information Technology, Industrial, and Healthcare sectors account for 45.1% of the portfolio value and just 33.2% of income. Meanwhile, energy and real estate account for 31.2% of income and just 20.3% of value.

On The Radar

Recent market volatility has resulted in some attractive valuations across the market. Here are a few names that I'm considering for future purchases.

Becton, Dickinson & Co (BDX) is trading near a 52-week low and near $200 for the first time since late 2017. It's grown EPS by 8% each of the last two years and is projected for high single-digit growth going forward.

It announced a 9.47% dividend increase in November, which was its largest increase since 2016. With a payout ratio below 30%, dividend growth will likely mirror earnings growth going forward.

Monolithic Power Systems Inc. (MPWR) is a company I've been watching for a while, and with the recent tariff tantrum, shares have finally dropped to a more reasonable valuation. The company now trades at just over 31X 2025 estimates, which is a 27% discount to its 10YR normal PE.

This seems a fair price to pay for a quality company with high-teens EPS growth.

The final stock to highlight is Intuit Inc. (INTU), which is an application software company known for its QuickBooks line of business products. The company is another consistent mid-teens earnings grower that is trading at a 17% discount to its long-term normal valuation.

Closing Thoughts

2025 is shaping up to be another year of volatility for world markets. Trade wars, high interest rates, and ongoing fears of a large-scale economic recession have stocks in correction mode, creating opportunities to buy quality companies at valuations not seen for a few years.

The DGI For The DIY portfolio has weathered the storm so far, although slower growth in the energy, consumer staples, and real estate sectors has slowed income growth over the past two years. However, the portfolio remains ahead of target and on a good path for continued growth.

A busy family life and work schedule have kept me from being as active as I once was on Seeking Alpha, but I still enjoy following the market and managing this portfolio. I've been providing updates on it for over a decade now, and hope to continue doing so in the years ahead.

I hope this update finds you well, and I wish you all happy investing for the rest of 2025.

data.title: Lovesac: Tariff Risk Not Adequately Considered As Company Launches New Couches
data.description:
It's been a long time since Lovesac (NASDAQ:LOVE) had a positive update for the markets, and yet that's what the premium modular furniture company delivered in mid-April with its Q4 earnings print and a robust outlook for FY25. Lovesac is one of the first consumer-products companies to issue an outlook after Trump's "Liberation Day" tariff announcement, and shares of Lovesac have jumped modestly since the earnings print: though the stock does still remain down ~20% since the start of January.

The question for investors now is: amid declining revenue and same-store sales growth now, can we really believe Lovesac's projections of returning to growth next year, especially when the cloud of tariffs hangs over the company?

I last wrote a bearish article on this stock in early January, when the stock was still trading in the mid-$20s. Since then, the company has continued to post negative comparable sales trends, while the new risk of tariffs has emerged and threatens to eat into the company's rich gross margin profile; which is one of the most unique features of this company versus many other consumer products manufacturers. With this in mind, I'm reiterating my sell rating on Lovesac.

The element that surprised the markets most was Lovesac's plan to return to revenue growth in FY26 (the year ending in February 2026), despite failing to achieve comparable sales growth this year. The company's outlook calls for $700-$750 million in revenue, which represents 3-10% y/y growth against $681 million in FY25 revenue.

The company is attributing the re-acceleration in growth to an aggressive product launch roadmap, including and especially its first couch product, dubbed "Evercouch."

And yet, there are the demand risks to consider. Furniture is a product that has long and discretionary replacement cycles: and amid macroeconomic conditions tightening (and the very high chance that furniture prices will rise, especially since so much of it is sourced overseas), fewer people will leap to replace their old wares. We note as well that recent bond market shocks have failed to bring down the 10-year yield, around which long-term mortgage rates are typically tied. With borrowing costs still high, we have still yet to reach normalization in the housing market (a sentiment echoed by Zillow (Z), which operates the largest real estate data platform in the U.S. and says that housing turnover is at historic lows), which in turn is dragging furniture sales.

To me, Lovesac's post-earnings rally may be a head fake, and we should be prepared for the company to cut its aggressive growth forecasts throughout FY25 as the macro reality sinks in. Steer clear and invest elsewhere.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Lovesac's latest quarterly results in greater detail. Broadly, I see a two major red flags for Lovesac: its inability to post positive comparable sales growth, plus its limited capital base.

The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Lovesac's revenge of $241.5 million declined -3.6% y/y to $241.5 million, though we note that this faced a tough compare from a 14-week quarter in the year-ago Q4 and also beat Wall Street's expectations of $230.3 million (-8.1% y/y) by a meaningful margin.

My bigger concern, however, is declining comparable sales. Sometimes referred to by other retail companies as "same store sales growth," this metric already adjusts for the 14-week compare and measures Lovesac's revenue growth only from locations that have already been open for 12 months or more, effectively excluding the impact of newly opened or newly closed stores.

Across all sales channels including online, Lovesac's comparable sales declined -9.4% y/y in Q4. This means that the average Lovesac store is losing a tenth of its sales. Effectively, Lovesac is only able to eke out low single digit growth because of its aggressive showroom expansion, adding 27 net-new locations (12% location growth) in FY25.

The key question to ask is if Lovesac can actually afford to keep up this pace of store openings while also devising workarounds for tariffs. As shown in the chart below, Lovesac's liquidity is quite limited, having only $83.7 million of balance sheet cash remaining plus $32.6 million of available capacity on its revolving line of credit, for around ~$116 million of total liquidity.

That's not much for a capital-intensive manufacturing business with a fleet of hundreds of showrooms. That liquidity cushion looks even thinner when we consider the fact that the near majority of Lovesac's products are foreign-sourced, and the company will have to either absorb a margin hit (which it is able to do, as its premium-priced products are still giving the company a very healthy 60.4% gross margin) or raise prices.

Speaking to the impact of tariffs and possible workarounds on the Q4 earnings call, COO Mary Fox remarked as follows:

Which brings us to the recent news on tariffs. Let's start with some contextual information. We have made significant progress in recent years, to diversify our countries of origin, and establish redundancy of each product, across multiple countries in order to have options. Prior to the recent news, our country of origin estimates for fiscal '26 were Vietnam about 50%, Malaysia about 28%, China down to 13% and Indonesia about 6%.

We are actively continuing this effort, with numerous options for additional diversification, including new geographies, and are working to get China to be under 10% of the total. As we learn more in the coming weeks, we will adjust accordingly, and have deployed task forces, to accelerate mitigating actions.

As Keith will outline momentarily, we enter fiscal '26, with higher than normal levels of inventory across our product lines, which was purposeful just in case of this possibility. In addition, we have numerous ways to structurally manage through various tariff scenarios, but we need to be careful not to implement these in a knee jerk manner that, could confuse our customers and damage the brand.

These options include working with our long-term vendors for concessions, reviewing opportunities for surgical price increases, adjusting our promotional intensity and capturing other efficiencies. Another important consideration, is that our structurally higher gross margins than many other competitors, mean that the effective price increases needed, to offset the tariffs are relatively smaller. We believe, we have the ability to selectively take price increases, due to the strength of our brand, and the unique and compelling nature of our Design for Life products that, are loved by many."

All of the things on Lovesac's to-do list require meaningful capital. It needs to keep adding showrooms in order to sustain its growth and offset negative comparable sales. It's increasing its inventory to get ahead of tariffs, which also consumes cash and possibly raises the risk of obsolescence and write-offs. And, the company may also change up its supply chain and work with different vendors, which also requires upfront investment. The latest news on Vietnam, Lovesac's largest country of origin at 50% of its supply base, was positive - and also perhaps why Lovesac shares rebounded post-earnings. But no concrete deal has been reached yet, and the time is ticking until the reprieves pass - which may cause fresh pain for Lovesac down the line.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices near $20, Lovesac trades at a market cap of $288.3 million. After we net off the $83.7 million of cash on Lovesac's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $204.6 million.

Against the midpoints of Lovesac's adjusted EBITDA guidance ($48-$60 million) and EPS range ($0.80-$1.36), Lovesac trades at:

  • 3.8x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA
  • 18.1x FY26 P/E

Lovesac's adjusted EBITDA multiple, sitting at a <4x multiple, is quite rare even in today's post-correction market. But to me, the company's outlook seems unrealistic. It's tough for me to bridge the gap from -9% comparable sales to a high range of 10% y/y growth in FY26, even after considering the possible contribution of a new couch product (which may also have some cannibalization impact on its current sectionals). In my view, it's highly possible that Lovesac's management hasn't fully de-risked its forecast for growing macro headwinds and possible tariff impacts.

All in all, the risk that's burgeoning in this stock isn't worth even the cheap price that Lovesac is trading at. I'd prefer to remain on the sidelines and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Housing Starts Plummet 11.4% In March
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

In the latest report by the Census Bureau, housing starts plummeted to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1.324 million in March. This marks an 11.4% decrease from February, the largest monthly decline in a year, but a 1.9% increase compared to one year ago. The latest data was below the forecasted rate of 1.420 million.

Background on Housing Starts

Housing starts track how many residential buildings began construction in the preceding month. The data is divided into three types of structures: single-family homes, residences with 2-4 units (condos or townhouses), and structures with 5+ units (apartment complexes). A critical aspect of the home-building industry is the powerful influence it has on the rest of the economy.

Here is the historical series for total privately-owned housing starts, which dates from 1959. Because of the extreme volatility of the monthly data points, a six-month moving average has been included.

Housing Starts: Structure Breakdown

In March, single-family housing starts were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 0.940 million, the lowest level since July. This represents a 14.2% decrease from February, the largest monthly decline since April 2020, and a 9.7% decrease from one year ago.

Multi-family buildings housing starts were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 0.371 million, unchanged from February and up 47.8% from one year ago, the largest annual increase since December 2021.

Note: Buildings with 2-4 units only have N.S.A. data available because tests for identifiable and stable seasonality do not meet reliability standards.

Housing Starts: The Population-Adjusted Reality

Here is the data with a simple population adjustment. The Census Bureau's mid-month population estimates show substantial growth in the US population since 1959. Here is a chart of housing starts as a percent of the population. We've added a linear regression through the monthly data to highlight the trend.

Housing Starts: A Footnote on Volatility

The extreme volatility of this monthly indicator is the rationale for paying more attention to its six-month moving average than to its noisy monthly change. Over the complete data series, the absolute MoM average percent change was 6.3%. The MoM range minimum was -26.4% and the maximum was 29.3%. The latest month-over-month percent change is 11.4%.

For visual confirmation of the volatility, here is a snapshot of the monthly percent change since 1960.

A Long-Term Look: Residential Building Permits and Housing Starts

In addition to housing starts, the U.S. Census Bureau and the Department of Housing and Urban Development published their March findings for new residential building permits. Building permits typically preceded housing starts, offering a glimpse into future construction activity.

Despite the fact that both are monthly seasonally adjusted annualized rate (SAAR) series, they are exceptionally volatile and subject to extensive revisions. Thus, it is unwise to assign much significance to a single month.

Over the long haul, however, the two offer a compelling study of trends in residential real estate, especially when we adjust the permits and starts for population growth.

Here is an overlay of the two series since the 1959 inception of the starts data and the 1960 inception of the permits data. The monthly data points are preserved as faint dots. The trends are illustrated with six-month moving averages of data divided by the Census Bureau's mid-month population estimates.

Here is a closer look at the overlay since 1990.

ETFs associated with home builders include: Invesco Dynamic Building & Construction ETF (PKB), iShares U.S. Home Construction ETF (ITB) and SPDR S&P Homebuilders ETF (XHB).

ETFs associated with residential real estate include: iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Apple: Overvalued And Overexposed To Tariffs Risks
data.description:
Investment thesis

My previous bearish thesis about Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) aged quite well as the stock returned -8% to its investors since mid-September, which is a weaker performance compared to the broader equity market.

The latest wave of the AAPL stock sell-off was triggered by President Trump's tariffs, which is indeed a substantial headwind for a company with a supply chain heavily tied to China. The bad news for Apple bulls is that the stock remains significantly overvalued as well. All in all, I reiterate a "Strong Sell" rating for AAPL.

Recent developments

The company released the latest quarterly earnings on January 30, with a double beat against consensus estimates. Revenue growth was quite modest at just 3.95% YoY, which will be even lower if we consider that there is inflation. Nevertheless, the company's EPS expansion capacity remains robust as the metric expanded YoY from $2.18 to $2.40.

Apple demonstrated operating leverage as the operating margin improved YoY from 33.76% to 34.46%. A significant factor of the EPS expansion was the decreased outstanding share count, as Apple is well-known for its aggressive stock buybacks. Everything might look very positive if we do not delve deeper. However, the devil is in the details.

In the revenue breakdown from the latest 10-Q report, we see that sales of the company's major revenue contributor, the iPhone, are stagnating. In addition, there is a breakdown from the Q1 report as of two years ago. We can see that iPhone sales have been stagnating over at least the last three years.

Moreover, we can also see that an impressive 15-16% YoY revenue growth demonstrated by Mac and iPad in FQ1 2025 is misleading. Both categories also showed revenue stagnation from 2021-2022, which is also a red flag.

In addition, the current situation with President Trump's tariffs will cause multiple headwinds for Apple. The company's supply chain heavily depends on China, meaning that the trade war with sky-high tariffs imposed by the U.S. and China will either weigh on the company's profitability or force it to pass on the massive inflationary effect on customers. Several prominent Wall Street analysts like BofA and Wedbush already shared their opinions that a "Made in USA" iPhone will cost substantially more.

Substantially increasing the selling price of the iPhone does not look favorable considering the rapidly intensifying competition in the global smartphone market. According to Canalys, Apple's global smartphone market share deteriorated in Q4 2024. If the iPhone becomes much more expensive it will be extremely difficult for Apple to maintain its market share.

There is another big potential drawback for Apple, which is likely to be caused by Trump's tariffs. Despite a 90-day tariff pause for European countries; it is apparent that relationships between Europe and the U.S. have become quite complicated due to tariffs. European consumers appear to be very sensitive to geopolitical trends when purchasing American brands, which we can see from Tesla's sales tanking across Europe. This is a potential risk for Apple as well, and the magnitude might be substantial, as Europe is the company's second-largest market. In addition, some experts say that tense relationships between the U.S. and Europe might push the latter closer to China. If this is the case, Chinese smartphone makers might flood the European market with their various brands, which already aggressively conquered Apple's market share in China.

Another notable indication that the worst might be yet to come is that there is no rush from insiders to buy the dip. AAPL is around 20% down YTD, which is a massive dip for the world's most valuable company. Nevertheless, insiders remain on the sidelines, which speaks volumes to me.

Valuation update

Apple has the lowest possible "F" valuation grade from Seeking Alpha Quant. I think that high valuation ratios are only justified when the company demonstrates strong revenue growth momentum and rapidly expanding profitability. That being said, with Apple's revenue stagnating over the last few years, I am highly doubtful that high multiples are justified.

The DCF model will help me in figuring out the extent of overvaluation. Apple's below $100 billion total debt is peanuts against the company's $3 trillion market cap, meaning that cost of equity is a good choice to discount future cash flows during the DCF analysis. This one is calculated using the CAPM approach, as all variables are easily available on the Internet.

The free cash flow margin assumption that I use should be adjusted to stock-based compensation [SBC] because it is a non-cash item. The ex-SBC FCF margin is 20.7%, which I expect to remain flat over the entire DCF horizon, as intensifying competition together with tariffs will weigh on the company's profitability.

The FY2025 revenue assumption is $407.92 billion. For the first scenario, I incorporated a 5% revenue CAGR for the next seven years. This is quite conservative, but in my analysis, I have highlighted that the company struggles to drive revenue growth as competition intensifies. In addition, Apple grapples against extremely high comparative figures. For these two reasons, I use a conservative 2% perpetual growth rate.

With a 5% revenue CAGR, the business's fair value is around $2.1 trillion. This is 29% lower than the current slightly below $3 trillion market cap. Incorporating an 8% revenue CAGR increases the company's fair value, but it is still 17% lower than the current market cap.

Overall, AAPL appears to be substantially overvalued, based both on the ratios analysis and the DCF modeling.

Risks to my thesis

The last few weeks showed us that the tariff rhetoric might be quite flexible, with some developments happening unexpectedly. First, there was a ninety-day "Reciprocal Tariffs" exemption for most countries except for China. Then, there was an announcement that various electronics [including smartphones] would be exempt from tariffs, which added a lot of optimism to AAPL bulls. However, on Monday, Trump said that electronics-related tariff exemptions will also be temporary. Therefore, the situation looks quite chaotic, which means that new exemption announcements are not impossible. Nevertheless, there is still substantial overvaluation and Apple's revenues started stagnating far before tariffs.

Furthermore, despite all of Apple's fundamental drawbacks, there is always an opportunity for a turnaround. The company already faced a creativity crisis during its vibrant history, and the iconic return of Steve Jobs with his iPod was a game changer that made Apple the most valuable company in the world. I think that the probability of such a scenario is extremely low because Steve Jobs was the "father" of this company, and he was an absolutely unique entrepreneur. Nevertheless, Apple has massive cash flow generating capacity and a strong balance sheet, which allows it to recruit the best engineers across the world and pay them superior salaries. Therefore, there will always be some possibility of a successful turnaround.

The company invests billions in R&D, which helps to release new products frequently. Apple recently unveiled the new MacBook Air with the cutting-edge M4 chip. The model offers a compelling 18 hours of battery life, a 12MP Center Stage camera, and enhanced support for multiple external displays. Simultaneously, the company also offered the iPad Air powered by the M3 chip. Thus, the company continues aggressively expanding its product portfolio, which increases the chances of releasing a new banger. On the other hand, these are not completely brand-new disruptive products but upgraded versions of products that demonstrated stagnating sales over the last few years.

The stock has been performing quite well during the May-August period, which is suggested by the last decade's seasonality patterns. This might be a short-term positive catalyst for AAPL.

Bottom line

To conclude, I think that at the current share price, AAPL is certainly a "Strong Sell". The company's top-line has been stagnating too long to call it just a coincidence or some temporary headwinds.

data.title: Netflix: Stock Price Jumps On Earnings Beat But Free Cash Flow Still Lags
data.description:
Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) again posted a sizable earnings beat that pleased Mr. Market. Even cash flow resumed growth after a down quarter. However, as I wrote in a Disney (DIS) article, Disney now leads in free cash flow. No matter what happens when Disney reports, the Disney business has a proportionately greater free cash flow margin as the streaming business is far smaller.

This remains a case where earnings growth over time far exceeds the cash flow growth. All one has to do is compare the earnings here to the earnings posted at Disney to see the difference in the free cash flow conversion percentage. Disney is far from alone in this advantage.

United Kingdom Example

The issue is that streaming itself is a part of the entertainment industry. No one has anything close to the control necessary to be considered the dominant factor.

The numbers shown above will vary from market to market. But clearly, Netflix has gone for market share over cash flow. Yet, as shown above, the share of this market is nothing close to dominant. Disney, with its smaller market share, has a model that is wringing out more free cash flow dollars than is the case for Netflix.

But that likely means that despite the size of Netflix and its market share, it's at a likely profitability disadvantage that will continue to require investments that take free cash flow shown in its calculations to catch up in profitability, whereas Disney has no such issues. Disney is free to grow the business to generate more profits.

The Main Problem

The major issue is that back when Netflix went public, management was focused on the virtuous cycle.

Back in 2016, management figured that the streaming model would work as shown in the figure above to produce a whole lot of cash flow by the time the company arrived at the size it is now (and hopefully long before that). It was several years before management gave up on that model, and even longer before there was a realization that the competition had a far better idea of different divisions to monetize hit series or franchises in multiple ways. As a result, the competition is in the monetization lead and is far more profitable with a lower market share.

This company remains dependent upon one hit after another to maintain its market share. If there is ever a hit "drought" there could be a severe price correction.

Latest Growth

Even the latest growth supplied by the company does not justify the current market price, let alone the reaction to the latest earnings report.

The growth compared to the previous year was shown as roughly 12%. Looking at the regions shown above, there's really nothing to indicate that will significantly speed up in the near future. To justify the latest price-earnings ratio, a growth rate in excess of 25% would be a minimal requirement. Normally, the price-earnings ratio should roughly equal the rate of growth. Here it far exceeds it.

If anything, as the company grows in size, growth will continue to slow.

Valuation

Yet, the price-to-earnings ratio would appear to factor in far more growth than management is projecting.

At a projected subscriber growth rate in the teens, it would likely take some time for earnings to properly support the stock price after the earnings reaction.

As long as the good news continues, Mr. Market can push the stock price up all he wants to. However, when the party is over, the downside is likely to prove to be substantial. All investors have to do is look at competitors like Disney and Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) that produce proportionately more cash flow compared to the streaming revenue because they monetize the content in several different ways. Those companies are not as highly valued as Netflix.

Cash Flow Issue

Worse yet, the cash flow is just barely below the net income. Classic business school estimation would be that net income plus depreciation should get the investor to GAAP cash flow. Now here, it will vary somewhat as the company shows the investment in new content along with the depreciation.

But the low free cash flow suggests that the roughly $30 billion in assets could prove to be an optimistic assumption of content worth, even considering that's a going concern evaluation. Without new content, that asset value has little hope of converting its value to profits.

Both Warner Bros Discovery and Disney have classics (franchises) that can be watched (and there is demand for) regardless of new content. They also both have content that's valued at zero (besides what is on the books) that there is demand for. This puts both of them at a far more conservative financial valuation than is the case here.

Summary

Netflix stock reached another high due to the great earnings report. But free cash flow is proportionately far below competitors like Disney and Warner Bros. Discovery, despite the fact that the market share is far larger here. That points to some unprofitable business here that needs closer examination.

Furthermore, Netflix has been making acquisitions with its free cash flow, which means even that cash flow is not really available to shareholders. This is an extremely large business for what cash flow is produced.

Management has long maintained that they have a quality priority. Only recently has free cash flow and GAAP cash flow moved up the priority list. Instead, this company has been about subscribers, earnings, and revenue growth. That implies some cost controls are needed while maintaining that quality priority.

The latest price-earnings ratio is far too high for the growth that the company has projected. Now the stock price could certainly go higher as long as the good news continues. But stocks like this tend to correct in such a way as to wipe out years of growth. Therefore, the downside far exceeds the upside potential, even if that downside does not happen for a while.

For me, that makes this stock a strong sell. A true disrupter, as Steve Jobs demonstrated at Apple (AAPL), generates a whole lot of cash. That is clearly not the case here (which is reason enough to watch from the sidelines).

For some reason, investors celebrate the earnings but do not care about the free cash flow. Cashless earnings are not an especially desirable accounting choice. That makes earnings comparisons challenging. For that reason, I prefer to compare GAAP cash flow and free cash flow.

At some point, the market will demand shareholder returns in cash. This company is not well positioned for that. Investors beware.
